section.title: Matthias Chosen
section.sectionRationale: Between the ascension and Pentecost, the waiting church devotes itself to prayer. Peter, reading the fall of Judas in light of Scripture, leads the believers to fill the vacant place among the Twelve. Two men are proposed, the church prays for God to show His choice, and the lot falls to Matthias, restoring the apostolic circle of witnesses before the Spirit comes.
section.thesis: The waiting church prays as one, and, guided by Scripture and by God who knows every heart, restores the circle of apostolic witnesses so that the number chosen to testify to the risen Christ is complete before Pentecost.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the gathered church waiting in united prayer; Peter's reading of Judas's fall through the Psalms and the need for a replacement witness; and the prayerful choice of Matthias by lot to complete the Twelve.

units.label: The waiting church at prayer
units.note: with one accord, continually in prayer

verses.text: Then they returned to Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives, which is near the city, a Sabbath day’s journey away.

When they arrived, they went to the upper room where they were staying: Peter and John, James and Andrew, Philip and Thomas, Bartholomew and Matthew, James son of Alphaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas son of James.
With one accord they all continued in {{proseuche|prayer}}, along with the women and Mary the mother of Jesus, and with His brothers.

units.label: Peter reads Judas's fall through Scripture
units.note: the Scripture had to be fulfilled

verses.text: In those days Peter stood up among the brothers (a gathering of about a hundred and twenty) and said,
“Brothers, the Scripture had to be fulfilled that the Holy Spirit foretold through the mouth of David concerning Judas, who became a guide for those who arrested Jesus.
He was one of our number and shared in this {{diakonia|ministry}}.”
(Now with the reward for his wickedness Judas bought a field; there he fell headlong and burst open in the middle, and all his intestines spilled out.
This became known to all who lived in Jerusalem, so they called that field in their own language Akeldama, that is, Field of Blood.)
“For it is written in the book of Psalms: ‘May his place be deserted; let there be no one to dwell in it,’ and, ‘May another take his position.’

units.label: Matthias chosen to complete the Twelve
units.note: a witness with us of His resurrection

verses.text: Therefore it is necessary to choose one of the men who have accompanied us the whole time the Lord Jesus went in and out among us,
beginning from John’s baptism until the day Jesus was taken up from us. For one of these must become a {{martys|witness}} with us of His resurrection.”
So they proposed two men: Joseph called Barsabbas (also known as Justus) and Matthias.
And they prayed, “Lord, You know everyone’s heart. Show us which of these two You have chosen
to take up this ministry and apostleship, which Judas abandoned to go to his rightful place.”
Then they cast {{kleros|lots}}, and the lot fell to Matthias. So he was added to the eleven apostles.

proseuche.gloss: Prayer, especially prayer addressed to God. The waiting church devotes itself with one accord to prayer as it awaits the promised Spirit. Prayer marks the newborn community from the first and frames every major step, including the choice of Matthias.

diakonia.gloss: Service, ministry, the work of a servant. Judas had shared in this ministry and then abandoned it; the vacant place is described as a ministry and apostleship to be taken up. The apostolic office is not status but service in the cause of Christ.

martys.gloss: Witness, one who testifies to what he has seen. The essential qualification for the replacement is that he accompanied Jesus from John's baptism to the ascension, so he can be a witness with the others of the resurrection. Apostolic testimony rests on eyewitness experience of the risen Christ.

kleros.gloss: A lot, or the portion assigned by lot. Casting lots was an Old Testament way of seeking God's decision, trusting Him to direct the outcome. This is the last time Scripture records the practice; after Pentecost the church is led by the indwelling Spirit.

basic.context: After Jesus is taken up, the disciples return to Jerusalem and gather in an upper room. About a hundred and twenty believers wait together, devoting themselves to prayer. During this waiting, Peter stands up to address a problem: Judas, one of the Twelve, has fallen away and died. Peter shows from the Psalms that his place should be filled, and the church chooses Matthias to complete the circle of apostolic witnesses before Pentecost.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the church's obedient, prayerful preparation for the Spirit. The main questions are: why does the number twelve matter, what qualifies an apostle, and how should we understand the casting of lots? Notice how Peter reasons from Scripture and how the church submits the decision to God, who knows every heart, rather than to mere human preference.
basic.originalLanguages: The believers continue in proseuche, prayer, with one accord. Judas had shared in the diakonia, the ministry, and abandoned it. The replacement must be a martys, a witness, of the resurrection. And the church discerns God's choice by kleros, the casting of lots, trusting Him to direct the outcome.
basic.typology: The twelve apostles answer to the twelve tribes of Israel; Jesus is reconstituting the people of God around Himself. Judas's betrayal and the filling of his place echo the psalms of the righteous sufferer, David foreshadowing the greater Son. The eyewitness testimony required here anchors the church on the historical reality of the risen Christ.
basic.christ: The whole concern is to secure witnesses to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus. The apostolic office exists to testify to Him. Even Judas's tragic fall is read as fulfilling the Scripture that the Holy Spirit spoke through David, showing that the events around Christ were within God's foreknown plan.
basic.god: The church prays to the Lord who knows everyone's heart and asks Him to show His choice. God is the one who selects His servants; the believers simply seek and submit to His will. Their united prayer and their trust that God directs the lot reveal a community that leans on God for every step.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 69 and Psalm 109 supply the texts Peter cites about Judas. Luke 6 records Jesus choosing the original Twelve. Proverbs 16 teaches that the lot is cast but its decision is from the Lord. Acts 6 shows the church again praying as it appoints servants for ministry.
basic.oneStory: Before the Spirit is poured out, the church completes the band of eyewitnesses who will carry the good news. This small, prayerful scene prepares the ground for Pentecost and for the mission that will run from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, all resting on faithful testimony to the risen Christ.

context.literary: This unit sits between the ascension and Pentecost, in the ten days of waiting. It falls into three parts: the gathered community at prayer, Peter's scriptural address about Judas, and the selection of Matthias. Luke frames it with prayer at the start and God's answer at the end, showing a church that acts under God's direction.
context.historical: The setting is an upper room in Jerusalem. The group numbers about a hundred and twenty, including the eleven apostles, the women who followed Jesus, His mother Mary, and His brothers. The parenthetical notes about the field of blood explain a detail already known in Jerusalem. Casting lots was a recognized way in Israel of seeking a decision from God.
context.canonical: The restoration of the Twelve links the church to Israel's twelve tribes and to Jesus' own choice of twelve apostles. The passage establishes the criteria of apostolic witness, grounding the New Testament proclamation in eyewitness testimony to the resurrection. It is the last biblical instance of casting lots, before the Spirit's coming changes how the church is led.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the waiting church as a praying, Scripture-guided community that completes the number of apostolic witnesses under God's direction, so that the foundation of eyewitness testimony to the risen Christ is secure before the mission begins at Pentecost.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what the early church did in a unique moment; it does not prescribe casting lots as the normal way to make decisions. The abiding lessons are the priority of prayer, submission to Scripture, dependence on God who knows the heart, and the grounding of the gospel in eyewitness testimony.

debates.question: Was the choice of Matthias right, or should the church have waited for Paul?

views.label: Matthias was rightly chosen
views.case: Peter acts on Scripture and the church prays for God to show His choice; God answers through the lot, and Matthias is numbered with the apostles. Luke records no rebuke, and the qualification, an eyewitness from John's baptism to the ascension, is one Paul did not meet in the same way.

views.label: The church acted hastily before Pentecost
views.case: Some argue the disciples should have waited for the Spirit and that Paul was God's intended twelfth apostle, since Matthias is never mentioned again. On this reading the lot belonged to the old order and the initiative was premature.

debates.bearing: The text presents the choice positively as prayerful, scriptural, and God-directed; whichever view one takes, the point stands that the gospel rests on eyewitness testimony to the risen Christ, and God, not human preference, appoints His servants.

study.originalLanguages: The community continues with one accord in proseuche, prayer, the atmosphere in which every decision is made. Judas had shared in the diakonia, the ministry, then abandoned it, and the vacancy is called a ministry and apostleship. The replacement must be a martys, a witness, who saw the risen Lord. The church discerns by kleros, casting lots, after praying to the Lord who knows every heart, trusting Him to guide the outcome, as Proverbs says the decision of the lot is from the Lord.

typology.type: The twelve apostles answering to the twelve tribes of Israel
typology.fulfillment: Jesus reconstitutes the people of God around Himself; keeping the number twelve signals that the church is the renewed Israel gathered by the Messiah.

typology.type: Judas as the betrayer of the righteous sufferer in the Psalms
typology.fulfillment: The psalms of David about his enemies foreshadow the betrayal of David's greater Son, and Peter reads Judas's end as their fulfillment.

typology.type: The eyewitness qualification of the apostles
typology.fulfillment: The gospel rests on those who saw the risen Christ, so that later believers, including us, receive a testimony anchored in real history.

study.christ: Everything here serves testimony to the risen Christ. The one qualification that matters is having accompanied Jesus and seen Him alive, so as to be a witness of the resurrection. Even the dark account of Judas is read in the light of Scripture that the Spirit spoke through David concerning the Lord's betrayer, showing that the events surrounding Christ unfolded within God's sovereign plan.
study.god: The church addresses God as the Lord who knows everyone's heart and asks Him to show His choice. God appoints His servants; the believers pray and submit. Their confidence that the lot's outcome expresses God's will, and their devotion to prayer, reveal a community that depends wholly on God as it steps into its mission.

crossReferences.note: May his place be deserted; Peter applies this psalm of the suffering righteous to Judas's fate.
May another take his position; the text Peter uses to justify filling Judas's place.
Jesus, after a night of prayer, chose the original Twelve, the circle now being restored.
The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the Lord, the assumption behind casting lots here.

study.oneStory: In the quiet days before Pentecost, the church prays and completes the band of eyewitnesses who will proclaim the risen Christ. This scene secures the foundation of apostolic testimony and shows a community wholly dependent on God, preparing the ground from which the gospel will spread from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: What were the believers doing as they waited between the ascension and Pentecost?

options.text: Devoting themselves with one accord to prayer.
options.note: Luke stresses that they continued united in prayer while they waited.

options.text: Gathering together in the upper room.
options.note: True, they gathered in the upper room, but the emphasis is on what they did there: they prayed with one accord.

options.text: Preaching publicly throughout Jerusalem.
options.note: That comes only after Pentecost; before the Spirit came they waited and prayed rather than preaching.

options.text: Arguing over who would be the greatest among them.
options.note: The text shows a united, prayerful community, not one divided by rivalry.

quiz.q: Why did Peter say Judas's place had to be filled?

options.text: Because Scripture foretold it, and a witness of the resurrection was needed to complete the number.
options.note: Peter grounds the decision in the Psalms and in the need for a full circle of eyewitness apostles.

options.text: Because the Psalms said another should take his position.
options.note: True and cited by Peter, but this leaves out the further reason, the need for a witness of the resurrection.

options.text: Because the church wanted to punish Judas by replacing him.
options.note: The motive was not punishment but fulfilling Scripture and restoring the witnessing Twelve.

options.text: Because Jesus had commanded them to elect a new leader immediately.
options.note: There is no such command; Peter reasons from Scripture and the church prays for God's choice.

quiz.q: What was the key qualification for the man who would replace Judas?

options.text: He had accompanied Jesus from John's baptism to the ascension and could witness to the resurrection.
options.note: Apostolic testimony required an eyewitness of the risen Lord.

options.text: He had been with the disciples during Jesus' ministry.
options.note: Close, but the requirement is more precise: an eyewitness all the way to the ascension who can testify to the resurrection.

options.text: He was the most gifted speaker among the believers.
options.note: Eloquence was not the criterion; eyewitness experience of the risen Christ was.

options.text: He came from a prominent family in Jerusalem.
options.note: Social standing had nothing to do with it; the qualification was firsthand witness of Jesus.

quiz.q: How did the church make the final choice between the two men?

options.text: They prayed to the Lord who knows every heart, then cast lots, and it fell to Matthias.
options.note: They asked God to show His choice and trusted Him to direct the lot.

options.text: They cast lots between Joseph and Matthias.
options.note: Accurate about the lot, but it omits the prayer that God would reveal His choice, the heart of the scene.

options.text: They took a majority vote among the hundred and twenty.
options.note: It was decided by lot after prayer, not by a show of hands.

options.text: Peter simply appointed Matthias on his own authority.
options.note: Peter did not choose; the church prayed and the decision was left to God through the lot.

quiz.q: What does this passage show about how the early church made decisions?

options.text: They sought God's will through prayer and Scripture, trusting Him to guide the outcome.
options.note: The church reasons from Scripture and submits the choice to God who knows the heart.

options.text: They followed the practice of casting lots for every decision.
options.note: This is the last recorded instance of casting lots; after Pentecost the Spirit leads, so it is not the abiding pattern.

options.text: They relied on Peter's leadership to settle the matter.
options.note: Peter did lead the discussion, but the decision was submitted to God in prayer, not settled by Peter alone.

options.text: They made choices by their own reasoning without seeking God.
options.note: The opposite is true: prayer and Scripture governed the whole process.

questions: The believers waited together in united prayer before the Spirit came. What does their patience teach you about the place of prayer before action in your own life?
Peter read the painful events around Judas in the light of Scripture. How might Scripture reframe a difficult or confusing situation you are facing right now?
The church asked the Lord who knows every heart to show His choice. Where do you need to stop leaning on your own preference and ask God to guide a decision?
The apostles were valued as witnesses of what they had seen. What have you seen and known of the risen Christ that you could testify to this week?
Judas abandoned a ministry he had shared in, while Matthias took it up. What ministry has God entrusted to you, and are you tending it faithfully?

translationNotes.note: A Sabbath day's journey is explained in a footnote as about 2,000 cubits, roughly 3,000 feet or 914 meters, the distance permitted for travel on the Sabbath.

These verses are Luke's parenthetical explanation for readers, describing the field of blood; the account complements Matthew 27, viewing Judas's end and the purchase of the field from different angles.
Peter quotes Psalm 69:25 and Psalm 109:8, drawing together two psalms of the righteous sufferer and applying them to Judas and to the filling of his place.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 1:12-26. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were skipped or treated as spaces when building verse text; the Psalm quotation in verse 20 was joined from its text elements. No em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
